claim: You’re listening to Naval Podcast, your authoritative source for new knowledge.
speaker: Nivi

claim: I have three frontier founders with us—three good-looking guys, actually, and a fourth good-looking guy, Naval.
speaker: Nivi

claim: He’s building Vercel into an AI cloud for the world of agents and whatever comes after that.
speaker: Nivi

claim: He’s building Boom Supersonic—supersonic aircraft, in his own factory, and jet engines as well.
speaker: Nivi

claim: He’s building a biohybrid brain interface that grows living neurons on silicon to restore sensory functions like sight—but eventually to explore new parts of the brain and new senses.
speaker: Nivi

claim: All three of these guys are not composing their products with off-the-shelf parts.
speaker: Nivi

claim: And we don’t care as much about what they’re building exactly as we do about what they’re learning about how they’re building.
speaker: Nivi

claim: What principles are they discovering that other founders can learn from?
speaker: Nivi

claim: What are they trying to figure out right now?
speaker: Nivi

claim: Naval, any reactions before I jump in to Guillermo?
speaker: Nivi

claim: I can’t remember my exact quote, but I’ve been really pilled with this idea of software factories.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
The job of the engineer being something where you just show up to work, you ship the output directly, and everything inside the company was—“how good is person A at shipping output B?”

And now what’s happening is, the way I’m judging you as an engineer is, “are you producing the factory that will produce multiplicative outputs B through Z?”

That’s a pretty significant change.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We used to believe—and it used to be somewhat controversial—that there are 10x engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Now clearly there’s 100x or 1,000x engineers, and the world hasn’t fully adjusted to this.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I used to get flamed on Twitter for saying there are 10x engineers, because it flies in the face of so much equality philosophy that everyone’s equal.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But the reality is, when you’re operating in idea domains, in intellectual and virtual digital domains, it’s not even 10x—it’s 100x or 1,000x, and it always has been.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The guy who invented JavaScript, the Brendan Eichs of the world.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Not to even mention—if you choose the right thing to work on versus the wrong thing to work on, that’s an infinity difference.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And it could just be not necessarily a better programmer, just one who had better judgment on what to work on in the first place.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And now obviously it’s less controversial because of AI leverage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: What’s controversial is the token leaderboards.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: People are still getting a little confused—“Well, I have a bunch of 100x engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
Look at all these tokens that I’m paying for.”

I’m curious if you guys have seen the same—how do you measure ROI?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s like the old measuring of lines of code.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Token consumption and lines of code feel like similarly not direct paradigms.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: My observation has been that Claude or ChatGPT is basically as good as you are in a domain.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you’re a really capable developer, these things are really powerful.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you’re a junior developer, you’ll find it to be more of a junior developer.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The feedback you give them sporadically seems to be incredibly important—these little updates seem to totally determine the types of performance you get out of them.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: There’s a new kind of support I give now—you come to me, you didn’t get good output out of the model, and I tell you what to prompt the model with.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The quality of the reprompting is extremely important.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To be clear, I think this will become less important over time.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: As the models get much smarter, you’ll be able to put in less and get more out.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But at this stage, it really seems to reflect back the judgment that the user brings in.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I’ve kind of resisted learning all the tricks and tips.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Always use plan mode.”

I just ignored all of that.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I assumed the model is going to get better faster than I would figure out how to use it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It would figure out how to use me faster than I would figure out how to use it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So I’ve just been completely ham-fisted with them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I get frustrated at them and have found myself typing less and less information, doing less and less work as time goes on, because I just assume I can brute-force my way through it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I’ll throw Codex, Claude, and Gemini at the same problem over and over and just waste tokens to save time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: No matter how expensive these models might seem, they’re still way cheaper than a human.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Don’t look at the tokens either as inputs or outputs.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Just look at your time, and look at the final output.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Even if they’re writing low-quality code—which I know in many cases they are—when the time comes and I want to ship to production, I’ll just throw more tokens at it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
“Go through, look at it, rewrite it.”

They’re just going to get better every generation.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: As long as we have verifiable domains and solved problems, they’re going to resolve those problems.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now in the unsolved problems domain—maybe you’re Terence Tao, at the cutting edge of creativity—you need to be working very collaboratively and carefully and closely with the model.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But I’m not at that level in software engineering.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Guillermo, you’re probably the most extreme software engineer on the team.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: How are you finding these models at the edge of their capability?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: There’s one thing that’s happened recently that resonates strongly with what you’re saying.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It used to be that you’d give a prompt to the model and it kind of does a classic next-token prediction thing and runs away with your idea.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Models now have been doing this intuitive planning mode—without you even having to ask them to plan—where it comes back to you and says, “Look, what you’re asking me for, there are these three routes we can take.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
Here’s the set of trade-offs.”

That’s the moment where people on X do the whole thing—“Now we have a PhD-level engineer model.”

The models at some point graduated.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Now they’re principal engineers, because they come back to you with a set of trade-offs.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: And obviously, sometimes they bullshit, which is hilarious—it tells you “this is going to take three weeks” and “this many tokens.” It makes really bad predictions.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But I respect the models a lot more as a peer that I’m going back and forth intellectually with.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: If you’re a really proficient engineer or architect, you’re still extracting more juice.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: So the question Max was positing—if you’re junior, do you get junior back?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Clearly not, because a junior gets more advanced knowledge in code than they would have been able to write by themselves.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But doesn’t an experienced architect get 10x where a junior engineer gets 2x?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I’m seeing this now with some of our junior software engineers on the team—what’s the next step in their career progression?
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s going from writing implementation for a feature to picking technologies.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Choosing between Postgres versus some other database.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Picking between ZeroMQ versus some other queuing system.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim:
The models can suggest them, but that’s the thing—you’ll see it and you’ll go, “No, no, I want to use this other thing.”

That’s the type of little feedback that really matters and the types of output you seem to get at this point.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: That said—you can ask the models “which one should I use and why,” and they know everything.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’ll give you a really good trade-offs matrix.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’d say, “Hey, go put this super-high-cardinality telemetry data into Postgres.” And it goes, “No, no, bro.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We don’t put that kind of data into Postgres.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
You should consider ClickHouse or Athena or whatever.”

That’s happened to me a lot.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The thing I’m still struggling with is—clearly the human is still completing the model.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
The human is the one starting to get the instructions back: “Go get me this API key, because it’s something only you can do.”

Or “Get me this amount of capital for my next set of investments.” You just watch.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Pretty soon every good SaaS company or hosting provider will have a CLI and API interface the models can use directly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: As long as it’s text-based, Unix-based—the agent can hack its own API.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And the money part—you insert crypto tokens, put in Bitcoin, put in whatever, and the model goes and pays for whatever it needs.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The thing I’m now thinking through is—is pure software dead?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Is pure software engineering an obsolete thing?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We had to learn code to communicate with them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now the models speak English—fuzzy, sloppy English, like a human—and they understand things.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You had to build hardware, and it was hard to build a software company alongside.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Patrick Collison says, “Software is art, and it’s hard to hire artists.”

Now, as a hardware founder—great, you can have really good software developed fairly quickly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you’re creating models, maybe that’s the new software engineering—training, tweaking, post-training, fine-tuning.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But classic software engineering—is that dead?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Is pure software something you can organize a company and a team around, and try to get some leverage?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Did you guys see—there was an article on X by Mitchell Hashimoto called “The Building Block Economy”?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: His argument is that the most useful thing for agents to have now is really powerful reusable building blocks.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To Max’s example, you wouldn’t expect your clanker to reinvent a queue infrastructure system every time it needs to send an email.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
It needs to bring in the right building block, right-sized for the task—“Okay, for this one it’s BullMQ.”

I challenge the notion that I’d want the agent to reinvent the entire universe from first principles in a way that’s incompatible with the rest of society and civilization.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s almost like reinventing highways, laws, policies—just for you.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
Even if there’s potential for extra optimization, there’s still cooperation-at-large-scale value of saying “we’re both depending on Postgres 13.2.”

The category of infrastructure software and building blocks these agents are going to use is—obviously in bias, this is what we’re building—extremely valuable.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I don’t see the agent reinventing all of that any time soon.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Another metaphor I’ve been using: anything that’s already been created that the models can reuse is like a token cache.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You don’t want to churn through a trillion tokens to reproduce what’s already existing.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s always a starting point the model can fork off from.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s going to change things quite profoundly.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: So these are like libraries and dependencies, but for models.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Through all of being a teenager and in my twenties, I’d get sucked into it and code for like twenty hours.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I haven’t written a single line of code in quite a while now.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But also—since December, I’ve built a huge amount of software that I now use every day.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: And I just can’t imagine going back to actually writing code by hand.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: I have a hard time seeing that as part of the future.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Anyone who understands what an API is, how data flows, inputs and outputs, performance—because you have to orient the model around “this is the level of expectation I have out of this operation.” That has always been infinitely more useful than writing code.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: A really proficient engineering leader has been quote-unquote vibe coding through people on Slack or one-on-ones—you’re transmitting your will, your intent, your experience, and letting others run with it.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I went from not having written code in twenty years to coding all the time now—through agents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It turns out that just understanding the basic principles of software engineering and algorithms gets you a long way.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The reason I stopped coding was that I didn’t have time to figure out the latest language, the latest architecture, the infrastructure pieces to plug into.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The thing that really changed is—it used to be you could build a lot, a lot would go straightforward, but then you’d hit some random thing and you could spend an indefinite period of time debugging some narrow thing.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Now, with agents, you just don’t get stuck anymore.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Hey Blake, how are you applying all this at Boom Supersonic?
speaker: Nivi

claim: It completely changes the role of software and hardware developers.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: From day one we tried to take a lot of traditional engineering workflows—hardware engineering workflows—and turn them into software.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you haven’t been around hardware engineering, let me try to make this clear.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: A lot of hardware engineering happens in Excel spreadsheets on engineers’ laptops in a silo.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Very complex spreadsheets, sometimes with VBScript code.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: All of this is actually software, but it’s treated as if it’s not.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There’s no source control, no automated testing.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you want to hand something off from an aerodynamicist to a structures engineer, that’s done manually with a spreadsheet over email.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: So we started building software frameworks to automate and make repeatable hardware engineering flows, with the idea that we could reduce the cost of iteration.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: But it was slow going—we could never afford enough software engineers.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What we’ve gotten into now is a mind-blowingly different model: the software engineers create the architectures, because they understand systems, algorithms, and division of concerns.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Then the hardware engineers can vibe-code their pieces because they know hardware engineering.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The result is mind-blowingly different productivity for small teams.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you’re designing a turbine blade—classically, a turbine blade starts cold, but when it runs it gets hot, so it gets bigger.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: You have to design both the aerodynamics and the structural design to work in its cold shape and its hot shape.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: You have to convert between cold and hot, between structures and aerodynamics.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: This takes one engineer one day for one blade for one piece of the analysis.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There are about a thousand blades in a jet engine.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Now, with a combination of software and hardware people creating the solution, you can change blade geometry and see in real time the structures and aerodynamics results.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Two engineers can design an entire jet engine.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: One of the things you mentioned is that software engineers are creating the tools and architectures for the rest of the engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: To me, that’s the biggest cataclysm of enterprise software—there’s no startup that builds hardware collaboration tools that can sell you anything anymore.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Internally, you’re just coding the right thing you need at any given time.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The reason spreadsheets were successful is that no one could build custom software.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The thing that approximates custom software the most is a spreadsheet with a bunch of VBScript functions.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I’ve personally moved almost entirely from Excel to Python models, where I can get believable simulations of things.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The thing AI hasn’t come to yet, but I think it will within the next year—probably within 2026—and that will be very exciting: right now it can generate software, but soon it will generate STEP files and PCB layouts.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: When it comes for mechanical and electrical engineering, that’s a whole other thing we haven’t seen yet.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: On the hardware side, this is a boon for all these little gadget companies and part companies that write really bad software because they can’t make great software.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now they’re going to be able to make good-enough software.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Or it may not even be software with a human front end—it might just be completely agentic, an agent accessing it, and you talk to it through voice to control hardware.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: This is one of the reasons China is big into open-source models.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re going all in on it because they have hardware superiority.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They have these very complex supply chains and component chains.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
They’re basically saying—“hey, if I can just generate software on demand, then I don’t have this disadvantage anymore against Silicon Valley.”

That’s not the only reason they’re doing open source.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re also behind, they’re distilling models, they’re catching up, they’re collaborating on resources.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But the Chinese government has a history of funding efforts that help their entire ecosystem along, especially in network-effect businesses.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They want to pool all their resources, catch up on AI, and use it to give their hardware stuff an advantage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Ironically, they’re doing all the open-source stuff because OpenAI is not open.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Grok publishes models, but they’re a model or two behind.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Google has some local models, nothing really competitive.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Anthropic, to my knowledge—I don’t even know of any open-source models from them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So all the open-source heft is coming from China.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It helps our hardware founders, but it helps their hardware founders and factories that much more.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: All the crappy little software that goes with all the random knickknacks and thingamajigs you buy off Amazon to tinker with on a lazy Saturday afternoon—that software’s getting a lot better very quickly.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Everyone’s had the wake-up call that without great frontier coding models, you don’t have self-improvement.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Imagine China as a whole not having the ability to produce frontier everything.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s not just about producing software—in any piece of this hardware pipeline, like Blake was saying, you need to generate software.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: If you fall behind in your ability to generate software, you fall behind in your ability to generate everything.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: One thing I’m curious about: everyone loves to talk about Chinese models.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: This is an argument I had yesterday at dinner.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: One person at the table was claiming you’ll just use DeepSeek for 97% of things because it’s so cheap, and if you need more intelligence you’ll just run it over and over again—the same problem.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’ll only use OpenAI, Anthropic, etc. for the most advanced tasks.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I was kind of like, “I don’t know.” I think intelligence is an unalloyed good.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: When these models make a mistake, you don’t know it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And it’s always cheaper than a real person, and real-time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you’ll just use the most intelligent model available.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Which isn’t great news, because it means you’ll end up creating a monopoly or oligopoly situation in AI.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But I always want the most intelligent programmer.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Given the amount of leverage I’m going to pour into it—through capital and code and people and marketing—I want to make the right decision every time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: When I have two models, one I know is a little smarter than the next, and they both give me answers, often I don’t actually know which is the correct answer.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So if I know one model is a little smarter, I’m going to go with that answer, and eventually I’m going to stop asking the model I think is less intelligent.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Have you guys found a use for these so-called less intelligent models?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We have AI Gateway data—basically every application agent goes through it.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s definitely usage of open models, but the top is heavily dominated by frontier intelligence.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s a caveat: frontier intelligence at reasonable cost and performance slaps at scale.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Gemini—people don’t get really excited about Gemini, but they put out models that are super smart at the right performance-cost combination.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: For a lot of tasks other than coding, interestingly enough, they’re the best industrial production models.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You can throw them at support tasks or browser automation.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: I’d always put a Gemini model there, and I’d look to Chinese models for those kinds of things.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: But any time I’m working to push the frontier, you need the best possible coding model.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Max, you’re pushing pretty hard into vertical integration and extreme urgency.
speaker: Nivi

claim: For many things, you can’t buy it, so you have to make it somehow.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We obviously don’t do this on things like frontier models—I have an Anthropic subscription.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We actually do use some of the Chinese models, to Naval’s point.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We have a big internal fine-tune of 3.2 that I use for a bunch of things—we’re going to look into porting to 4 soon.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But that’s on the personal side, not on the company side.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Our preference would always be to buy something.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If there’s a vendor that offers a service at a great price—for example, PCBs.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: You can buy them in unlimited quantity from Asia.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But the closer our products get to being a single block of covalently bonded matter, the better they’ll be.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Lower power, smaller, higher performance, longer lasting.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: In order to do that type of integration—to actually innovate beyond just piecing together things you can buy off the shelf, which is really very limiting—you have to learn to do it yourself.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: So we own a captive MEMS foundry on the East Coast.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: There was no other way to do the type of packaging and assembly we wanted to do.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: All of this is going to be affected heavily by AI over the next few years.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Ironically, one of the biggest impacts we’ve seen of AI inside the company is in regulatory interactions.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If we can generate documentation, or if we can ask—“we want to evolve this product, there are thousands of ISO standards that might apply, which ones do we have to comply with, trace this through”—that used to require a whole regulatory and quality team for several months.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: When I think about stuff like the surgical program or the MEMS fab—ultimately the software still needs hands.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s going to be smarter than us, but if it can’t make things, those are real boundaries.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: We’ve instrumented our foundry as well as many other parts of the company in ways where, as these models get better, that should show up pretty immediately in things like the cell engineering we’re doing and the material science we’re developing.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Our protein engineering group really uses deep learning a lot—I think we’re probably state of the art there.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It means different things in different parts of the company.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: What Max was talking about with regulatory stuff makes me realize—it’s been a while since I generated a basic legal document using a lawyer.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I stopped asking lawyers for NDAs, agreements for this, sign that, research this.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: There’s the old joke that law is like spaghetti code—very complicated code they try to put in English.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It contradicts this code over here, has to fit into that code over here.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: For junior engineers and junior engineering—junior engineers basically got a promotion to senior engineer, and junior engineering got taken over by agents.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The same way, in law, you can say “paralegals just got fired,” or you can say “paralegals just got promoted to senior lawyers, and now they can spend their time thinking about the law.”
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s actually interesting to think about the parallels between how software engineering is evolving and lawyers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You never know exactly what lawyers put into these documents—you just trust them.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Can you do red lines?” What you’re valuing in the relationship with a lawyer is that they’re a trusted authority.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: They’re putting their reputation on the line.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There’s a parallel with software engineering.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The biggest problem today is this mountain of slop that ends up as a PR.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There are all these memes on Twitter—“way back in the day we used to read every line of code of a PR.” Well, in my world—infrastructure—I want engineers to be able to say “I understand” every line of that PR.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: That doesn’t necessarily mean you’ve read every line.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It means you can say “I understand the consequences of this PR.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
I’m signing off on understanding the consequences.” Or, “I wrote the test harness, the simulations, the proofs, the type-checkers—even without reading this, I have confidence I can sign off that it’ll be safe in production.”

There’s a world in which we embrace that everything is going to be spaghetti code we don’t fully understand, but we write the evaluators that give us confidence, and we rely on people—the infrastructure production engineers—to say, “Okay, I’m fine sending this into prod.” Someone is going to get paged if your systems go down.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Another thing people are underestimating: creating software is really easy, zero to one.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: And are you still motivated to invest all those tokens in maintaining it in prod?
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: That’s how we train these models—with good verification data—and now we need human verifiers.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: A lot of the old function of people, lawyers, engineers, operations people, moves to verifying the stack and saying, “Yeah, this is roughly correct, I’ll roughly stand behind it, I’ll support you if it goes wrong.”

claim: One of the things we’ve seen related to regulatory — it massively reduces change aversion and improves iteration.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Example: let’s say you’re going to certify an airplane.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: One of the zillions of things you have to do is prove it can withstand a lightning strike.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The regulatory documentation for the test plan stretches on for, say, 200 pages.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What you would classically do is hire a — let’s be honest — not super-bright engineer who’s willing to be there, monkey at keyboard, writing 200 pages of regulatory compliance documentation.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: And by the way, if you change the airplane, now you want to cry, because there’s another two months of rework of this rote compliance documentation.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What we’ve found is we can build a RAG that will enable us to basically prompt our way through all of that work in — let’s call it minutes.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The first-order effect is you save a lot of time.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The second-order effect is, if you change the specification of the airplane, it now takes minutes, not months.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: And the third-order effect is you can get rid of the not-very-great engineers and have a small number of really creative ones who can iterate rapidly, because the cost of change goes down.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: In a certain sense, the entire regulatory burden — which really hurts the ability to iterate — drops away.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: This is a really undersold story in AI right now.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The consensus in Silicon Valley is that regulation sucks — we want to go faster, we want to realize this amazing future, we want abundance, prosperity, and stuff that slows down that future is to be avoided.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s totally crazy what goes into building any physical thing in a lot of places.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But a lot of the regulations themselves are not the problem.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you’ve actually read a lot of these things — having non-smog-choked cities is great.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The problem is that it’s really difficult for humans to deal with understanding and complying with this, and every time you have to exchange a letter with the government, you wait months.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If you could take a lot of the things we’ve learned and make them totally frictionless, that would be pretty cool.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Until the regulator starts spewing tokens back at us.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Then you start getting huge amounts of documents from the regulators that you have to comply with, and it’s agent-on-agent wars.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I’d actually argue that would be an improvement from where we are now.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: One of the terrible things right now is, if you’re going to build anything physical, you have to get a building permit.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The worst thing we’ve run into is the fire department, because they have the moral imprimatur of people pulling people out of burning buildings — and yet what they actually do is just screw with your design for buildings for months.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If we could replace the fire marshal with an agent that would critique your building plan quickly — even if its feedback were overdone — it would be massively better than the delays that exist today.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: When Max was talking about this potentially being a good thing — that we have all this regulation — my head went to: the thing that makes agents successful is humans or other agents setting up the right testing guardrails.

claim: People are really excited about slash goal, or Ralph loops, where you tell the model, “Go do this, and this is your exit criteria.” I’m telling Blake, “Go make us all supersonic.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Your exit criteria is that you’ve complied with all of these regulations.” There’s totally a world where we say the regulations are great — they’re like our test suite.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: As long as passing them doesn’t incur contradictions, and the regulations are actually reasonable, they’re an awesome guardrail.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Otherwise we’d be shipping slop directly into the air.

claim: This is going to turn into a Red Queen’s race.

claim: They’re going to have agents, we’re going to have agents.

claim: I’m sure the patent office is drowning in spam.

claim: They’re going to get DDoSed by clever entrepreneurs just overloading them with documents.

claim: It’s possible the approval time for this stuff may extend out as it suddenly gets flooded.

claim: First, Europe — not really better than the FDA, but they have a different system.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They’ve got these notified bodies — basically private businesses blessed by their host governments to certify things.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The notified-body system creates slightly better incentives at the review layer because they can hire people, they can grow, there’s competition.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They themselves have to be compliant with conditions placed by host governments, but it means there can be many thousands more reviewers than in the US.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Second — there actually is one approved, getting-paid implantable BCI today, which is in China.

claim: They have a system that I think is going to give us a run for our money if we’re not careful.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The costs to bring a drug or device to market are just much lower.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: You can try things in humans and try things on market.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Here’s the thing I’ve been spending a lot of time thinking about.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Twenty years ago we were buying far fewer laptops and phones; each one was much more expensive.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Now they’re cheaper, there are far more of them, we buy more of them, total spending has gone up.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Stock prices of Qualcomm and Samsung and Apple are way up.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: They’re using the excess wealth generated by phones and laptops to buy more phones and laptops.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Because of the reimbursement mechanism — there’s this enterprise sale happening — the bucket of money we use to buy healthcare is basically fixed.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It is not increasing as there’s more stuff producing better healthcare outcomes, the way we see in technological growth industries.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The rate of spending on healthcare grows at roughly the rate of growth of tax receipts.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: If AI is booming and there are major advances, and two years from now we’re spending ten times as much on AI, this could be great.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: But if in two years we’re spending ten times as much on healthcare, this would be a catastrophe.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: This is fundamentally at odds with being a technological growth industry.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: There’s this omni-problem in healthcare, all related to the same thing: it’s just too expensive to bring these things to market.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The way out of this is not single-payer or some revision to health insurance.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: It’s to bring down the costs so that someone can buy this with a credit card, finance it, maybe like a car, worst case — and then you charge them in the transaction.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: To do that, we have to make it cheaper to bring these things to market.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: That will allow them to sell these things for $10,000 instead of $100,000.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Fundamentally, there’s no private market in healthcare.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The analogy people make sometimes — imagine that instead of going to restaurants and paying, you’d go to all the restaurants, and at the end of the month you’d send all the receipts and bills to your insurer or to the government, and they would reimburse you.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: There’d be a line outside every good restaurant.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’re basically running a small communist society inside a larger capitalist society.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s also what we’re doing on roads, which is why we have traffic.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: There’s no variable pricing for getting on the highway, which is why it’s always clogged.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: If you want to step on the third rail of healthcare for a moment, think about this plan.

claim: Imagine that the first 20% of your annual income was your healthcare deductible.

claim: Whatever your annual income is, the first 20% is your healthcare deductible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The rest is paid by the government and the insurance system, up to the usual caps they have today.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’d create a private market pretty quickly.

claim: Right now, very wealthy people can spend voluntarily into the system.

claim: This is where AI should really shine, and democratize what you can actually do when you find yourself in that situation.

claim: How much autonomous software do you have in your organizations that’s running on its own, or near-autonomous and improving on its own?
speaker: Nivi

claim: We have a capability that fires off upon finding anomalies—I recommend everyone create a version of this, or Vercel offers one.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Today most engineering organizations respond to anomalies by setting up alarms or monitoring thresholds by hand, which is pretty insane, but that’s how the entire industry works.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We’ve automated a lot of the SRE job—Site Reliability Engineering.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Any metric that slows down, speeds up, or changes throughput fires an anomaly alert, an agent investigates, and the agent can decide to create an incident.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: If an incident is filed, people get looped in and the agent begins remediation.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We’re doing everything except giving the agent the tools to change prod—we’re serving solutions on a silver platter to engineers.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The other thing working really well: autonomous optimization and autonomous security research.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It’s incredible—like Mythos, but you get it today.

claim: We run it against our entire monorepo using ten thousand concurrent agents in the cloud.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: It found several quarters’ worth of security-research progress in a couple of days, for fourteen thousand dollars of tokens—months of red-teaming, entire teams of people.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Cybersecurity is becoming a nightmare: too many vulnerabilities, too much work, adversaries too powerful.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: You’ve probably seen people on Twitter translating codebases from one language to another—once you’ve done the work to get a working program, optimizing or rewriting it in a native language is now quite doable with frontier models.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: Just from my own vibe-coded app—I built a bug-reporting queue for my TestFlight users.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I stopped all project work across the entire company for a week and said, “Everybody, from the receptionist to the engineers, build whatever you think is the most important thing to build.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Your only requirements: you have to use AI, and you have to demo it for the whole company when you’re done.” I expected a large number of silly projects and a small number of needle-movers.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: We got the opposite—a large number of needle-movers and very few silly projects.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Two or three were trajectory-changing; they’d absolutely change the direction of the company.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: What surprised me most: the receptionist—the ship-and-receive associate whose job was to take packages off a truck and email people when their stuff hit inventory—built an automation for that.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: The conclusion I came to: everybody has some idea of what could exist that would make the world better, but their first-order ideas are often stupid, and they can’t project that out and see it.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: But if they can go from idea to an actual thing, they can react and iterate.
speaker: Blake Scholl

claim: Give them a week, and by the end they’ve built something that makes sense.

claim: You can argue nature is art—a sunset—but that’s pure intelligence working without motive, so no ego gets involved, and your brain recognizes the complex system.

claim: Art in the human sense is: someone felt something and wanted you to feel it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So a beautiful photo—if a person takes it versus AI generating the exact same photo down to the last pixel, the person taking it has more meaning for you.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: That was one of the first times I looked at this and thought it was really cool.

claim: A human came up with the training and the prompt to arrive at that riddle.

claim: It’s possible AI does that itself in the future, but I give whoever came up with that optical-illusion ControlNet idea the credit.

speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Nobody wants to see another Studio Ghibli work again.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Humans are the ones who generate surprise completely out of the data distribution, and they do it with intent—and intent matters for meaning.

claim: Take an AI trained to be perfect at mathematics, within the formal system.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: That kind of thing I don’t think an AI can get to.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The meaning comes from the fact that a human did it for a purpose and conveyed something.

claim: The really deep question: is it possible for an LLM or transformer to go out of distribution—to have a new idea that wasn’t present in the training set?
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: The training sets are so large it’s hard to imagine ideas that aren’t in them somewhere.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But if they exist, they probably lie in the natural domain—physics, interaction, feeling, emotion, evolution—things language isn’t subject to.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: There are still things outside of language, though language is a great compressor of a lot of it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I think the question is how you go out of distribution without randomness.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: In reinforcement learning you can sample an action from a distribution and get randomness that walks you into new territory.
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Can humans go out of distribution—where does any new idea come from?
speaker: Max Hodak

claim: Natural selection works through pure randomness—mutate a gene and see what happens.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But humans seem able to cut through infinite space, eliminate huge swaths, so our creativity makes sense within the larger scheme.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Maybe AI is starting to do that at the edges, as we’re seeing with some math problems—but math is a very bounded domain.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: At the moment, truly stepping outside and surprising people is still the domain of humans.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Human without AI, forget it; pure AI isn’t there yet—but human plus AI, we’re in that era, and I’m betting we stay there longer than people think.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Humans will have an enormous amount of value—more value.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Everyone here, our productivity has gone through the roof.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Basic economics says that when productivity is higher, you’re wealthier and you hire more people, not fewer.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If someone’s really good with AI and really smart and creative, I want to hire them more than ever, for the leverage.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We’re hiring juniors and super-seniors, as long as they’re really good with agents and quick to adapt.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: My hypothesis is we end up with a larger number of smaller teams.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: The number of people required for any given task drops a lot.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim:
People who only see first-order effects say, “All the jobs disappear—I can do a jet engine with two people, not a thousand; 998 jobs gone.”

But what it actually means is you can create a lot of different jet engines.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: We’ll get an explosion of entrepreneurship, an explosion of founders, and a very large number of very small teams.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: AI provided base-level intelligence and domain knowledge and cut through the jargon; now agents provide a lot of agency.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So what’s left is creativity, taste—and yes, you need enough agency to get started and to stick with it, but you don’t need to spend twenty years learning one thing before you can contribute.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: That barrier going down means generalists are having a field day.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: At the end of the day we’re all generalists—we like to think about everything.

claim: Max is here talking about consciousness and the FDA and brain science and creativity.

claim: The people on Twitter fond of saying “experts, credentials, sources” are the ones getting hurt, because the expertise matters less now.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You spend five or ten years getting a PhD—hopefully it developed your creativity, instincts, taste, and judgment, because if all it did was help you memorize jargon and scaffolding, AI cuts right through that.

claim: So it’s people with AI versus people without AI—and the single best thing you can do for yourself is get really good with these tools, and always know the edges of what they can and can’t do.

claim: For a lot of tasks other than coding, interestingly enough, they’re the best models.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: So the question Max was positing—”if you’re junior, do you get junior back?” Clearly not, because a junior gets more advanced knowledge in code than they would have been able to write by themselves.
speaker: Guillermo Rauch

claim: There are only two skills you have to have in life: How to build and how to sell.
speaker: Nivi

claim: Today we’re going to talk about the sales side.
speaker: Nivi

claim: Naval is a particularly gifted seller, and some people have accused him of having his own reality distortion field.
speaker: Nivi

claim: I just try to figure things out, and then if there’s something I believe in, I try to convey that as accurately and honestly as possible to the other person.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I think the full extent of my sales training is that I watched Glengarry Glen Ross.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I read Robert Cialdini’s Influence—his original book on persuasion.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It has one good idea in there, which is anchoring, but that’s it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Robert Cialdini popularized CLASSR—which is consistency, liking, authority, scarcity, social proof, and reciprocity.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Those are like the six ways in which you influence people.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And then in his subsequent book he added a seventh, which was basically anchoring.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you feel like someone is selling to you, and if you feel like you’re being sold, it’s a turnoff.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Humans are hardwired to resist being sold to.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So I think what matters much more is credibility.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Credibility is way more important than sales.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you want to be good at “sales,” then really what you want is you want to be credible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You want to be the real estate agent that steers people away from bad deals and bad neighborhoods and bad houses, so that when the right one comes along for them, they trust you.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You really have to take your ego out of it, put yourself in the other person’s shoes and figure out what they want.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
And then once you’ve figured out what they want, then you can “make the sale.”

But I’m not the guy you would talk to for making the sales quota, where you have to sell like 50 pieces of software this week or this month.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But what I can tell you is that the people you most want to impress in life are the ones who can see right through you.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And they will see right through your sales tactics; they will see right through your pitching them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So if you want to work with the people who are at the top of the top, if you want to successfully sell the biggest things that are out there, then you have to be very credible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And to be credible, you have to be authentic.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They have to be able to trust you, and that you’re not just doing what’s expedient for you—you’re thinking about the long term.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You understand what you’re talking about, and you can explain it simply.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re not going to come up to speed quickly enough, so you do have to figure out how to analogize it and convey it to them, and it doesn’t always work.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I know there is a model out there that does work, where some people are incredibly aggressive.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Just keep beating on it, and eventually you’ll break through.”

I’m not one of those people—I’m just lazy.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I like to go where it’s easy, so if my “sale,” or if my pitch does not resonate with somebody, I move on.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I move on instantly, and I just keep looking for the person whom it will resonate with.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: That’s a technique, and there are times when I do contradict people outright.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And it’s selfish honesty because I want to make the right decisions for myself, which means that I have to try to be as objective as possible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I’d just be walking around with blinders on and probably be wrong 95% of the time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The next technique I’ve noticed in Naval is that he is truthful and positive at the same time.
speaker: Nivi

claim: If you ask me for advice, I can be truthful or I can be positive, but it’s incredibly difficult, and it’s a really rare skill to be able to do both at the same time.
speaker: Nivi

claim: It takes a lot for me to fire someone, and I’ve gotten better at it over time, but I’ve historically been very bad at it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: In most companies I’ll lean on my co-founders to be the ones doing that.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So when it’s time to fire someone, even though they might be a drag on the team or it’s not the right place for them, I’m empathic.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And really the only way I’ve found to fire people that works for me is I have to be convinced that they’re going to be more effective somewhere else in some other role, and then I have to help them find that role or at least be available as a reference for them on that role.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: By the way, when I say truthful and positive, what I mean by positive is that you leave the conversation with Naval energized to actually work on the discussion you just had.
speaker: Nivi

claim: And I think leadership is one of the core, core things that you have to drive in an organization.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You just have to inspire people to want to do the work.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you do have to take the time to listen to what those people want, and then figure out where there’s an overlap in what you want done, and then inspire them to go do it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Instead, teach them to yearn for the vast and endless sea.”

So if I’m recruiting someone, I’m always pitching them on what I think is correct—it’s honest; it’s authentic—which is that startups are just a much better way to build businesses, to live your life, and much more fun to work in than large companies.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And yes, in the short term it’s grueling, but in the long term, once you’ve done startups, it’s very difficult to go work for a big company.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Once you have worked with a lot of autonomy or worked for yourself, it’s very hard to be employable in a traditional sense.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I think one of my most popular tweets was, “A taste of freedom can make you unemployable.” And that’s kind of what I mean, especially for talented and skilled people who are self-motivated.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And you’re not going to do your best work when you’re not free.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you want to be in the minimum-sized group to do the scope of the job that you set out to do.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We are designed to go on missions with small groups: everything from people jumping on a boat and sailing to the West Indies or to the New World, or people going to war, or people going to build a business.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So when you read these stories like Liftoff, which is a story of Musk and his team building the first SpaceX rocket, or The Macintosh Way, which is about Jobs and his team building the first Macintosh—and there are other various books, like there’s Soul of a New Machine, etc.—these are incredibly inspiring.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Because I think deep, deep, deep down, this is what every person really craves, which is if you’re building something, you’re in that kind of world where you want to be with a small team of highly competent people, each pulling their weight and doing their job to the best, such that you can completely rely on them, and then you get to do your creativity at the max.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And then at the other side, you get this incredible outcome.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And that’s more of the model in normal society.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you see low trust societies, where people don’t trust each other, or where the structure makes it impossible to work together—the taxes are too high, the regulations are too high, there’s too much bureaucracy—things are illegal.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: People know each other in the social network; they can check each other out.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And then you can get together these teams of 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 people who can do what was considered impossible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You want to function with high-trust people in small groups to solve really hard problems.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And so when I’m “pitching” people on joining a company, that’s what I’m pitching.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
This is where you have the most fun.”

One of the recruiting tricks that I use is—you’ve got to keep the bar super high.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The next technique I’ve noticed is that you don’t sell anything that you’re not inspired about.
speaker: Nivi

claim: I asked Jeff Fagnan from Accomplice—a friend of ours— what he thinks about your sales skills, and he described it as an “evangelical sale.”
speaker: Nivi

claim: If you’re not excited about the thing, what are you doing selling it?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s a miserable life if you’re selling things that you don’t care about.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
I know in The Wolf of Wall Street and all those kinds of movies, it’s lionized like, “Hey, sell me this pen.”

You know, “Sell me this fork.”

I don’t care about selling you this pen or selling you this fork, unless I believe it’s the best pen ever made.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If I believe it’s the best pen ever made, I’ll sell it for free.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So I think for me, sales is a byproduct of credibility, and obviously it helps to be articulate and it helps to be empathic.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: In terms of the actual sales skills, I know the whole Cialdini checklist.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: All frameworks in life—whether it’s like your working out framework or your sales framework, or even your building an app framework—they’re all secondary, and distant secondary, to your motivation.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: One of the reasons why I think my cryptic tweets work better than formulas is because they’re really about inspiration and motivation.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you’re the kind of person who needs to read motivational tweets and listen to motivational podcasts all the time, then you’re probably not going to make it either, because you have to be self-motivated at some level.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And if that fire can’t be lit, then you’re just going to be listening to motivational podcasts until the end of time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So closing the loop on vibe coding—vibe coding is just my latest obsession.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Every six months I get obsessed with something new, and I’ve learned over time, there are bad obsessions—like, if you are overeating, or if you’re doing drugs, or you’re playing too many video games, or anything like that—that’s a bad obsession.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If I’m into some piece of technology, if I’m loving some podcast where I might be learning something, if I’m getting into some specific kind of workout, I feed that obsession.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And then once the obsession phase passes, like it inevitably does—you get a little tired of the thing—some large piece of it stays with you for the rest of your life, and you just learn how to work it into your everyday psyche and personality and being.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Like if you want to be good at sales, then find something that you really care about that you want to sell and go sell that thing.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If it feels to you like you’re selling, then you’re probably selling the wrong thing.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But if you’re just being enthusiastic about it, if you’re just conveying your enthusiasm, and you can’t control yourself, then you found the right thing to sell.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So if you’re in the sales business right now and you’re selling something you don’t care about, you need to go find something else to sell.

claim: Let me give you the full text Jeff sent me about your evangelical sales.

claim: Takes you through the bigger picture then makes you want to do the small things.

claim: He’s so passionate—whether it’s a toothbrush, restaurant, or a shitty startup.

claim: If you want to be genuine, if you want to be taken seriously by the people, again, who can see right through you, it’s important not to overthink these things.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Because, for example, if I’m raising money for a company—like so for my new company, Impossible, we’ve raised a round already and we’re probably going to raise another one at some point—the way I approach that fundraising is I’m not driven by an external clock.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Let me put the deck together now and go pitch it.”

I actually wait.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And I measure my own excitement level, and when my own excitement level gets above a certain threshold, then I know I’m ready to go raise the money because now I’m genuinely excited about where the business is, and I feel like the business really deserves another round of financing.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: At that point, it’s not hard for me to go and sell it, and at that point I just have to go find a bunch of people, explain it to them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And if they don’t get it, that’s their loss—I just move on to the next one.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I don’t fixate on the person who didn’t get it, because most people won’t get it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But it’s much more about measuring my own internal excitement level based on the fundamentals of where the business is at.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And if I go out to raise money, that means the business is at the point where I’m excited enough about it that I now just have to genuinely convey what I already see and know to be true.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I don’t have to pitch something that isn’t true.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I have to just genuinely convey what’s down there that’s getting me excited.

claim: If Naval’s going to pitch you on something, or communicate, or persuade, there’s always going to be a preamble.
speaker: Nivi

claim: There’s going to be a larger context that he sets, whether it’s the problem situation, a story, a historical setting.

claim: If I’m raising for a company, I’ll start raising months and months before we need it—preferably six to 12 months before we need it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If it’s a kind of deal that’s easy to unwind, if it doesn’t work out, then sure, you can be a little more forgiving about it.

claim: A contract is when two parties agree to constrain their future outcome in exchange for working together.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Then, in exchange, because we’re now constrained to work together in a certain way, we create something of greater value, and we can share the spoils from whatever we create.”

It’s a stag hunt.

claim: So you have to be convinced that this is one of the better paths.

claim: Founders end up getting fired or leaving based on this.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
It’s always more like, “I can keep helping you if we can work this out.”

So you’re focused on what you can give them in the future.
speaker: Nivi

claim: The upside is always bigger in the future, and the upside can be 100x, 1,000x, 10,000x bigger.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’re not looking for the company that gets sold early.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The same way when you’re doing deals, the most important thing is your time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You have to keep your time and your optionality available.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So if you’re going to give that up, it only makes sense to do that for something that has that opportunity to be 100x or 1,000x on your time or on your capital.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now, sometimes the big spoils come out and then the knives hit the table, and then you see people get greedy.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Unfortunately, business is war by other means, and so that does happen.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: A lot of times you will get ripped off and you do have to stand up for yourself—sometimes just on principle so that you don’t get taken advantage of in the future.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But generally speaking, I think you want to focus on the upside and you want to preserve your time and your reputation and your mental health—your peace.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But if you feel like, “I can walk away from this and I can recover my time and I know not to work with these people again,” or “I know that these stakes are too small to bother fighting over,” then you just don’t do it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Generally, I think people spend too much time splitting hairs on small stakes and not enough time focusing on the big upside.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And that pattern repeated multiple times across other funds that I ran of different types and different structures and different industries.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But all of that said, I don’t believe in just blindly pursuing something or fighting over something or splitting something or doing some deal.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And I believe that even if it may not work out the absolute best, it’ll work out fine.
